title: Education Cultures
summary: Builds the conditions for educators and educational innovation to thrive, demonstrate impact and shape the sector.
detailSummary:
Education Cultures will make King’s a place where educators are better able to lead, improve and influence education. Colleagues will have clearer opportunities to develop ideas, build confidence, access support, demonstrate impact and gain recognition for their contribution. Strong practice will be easier to surface and spread, while educational leadership and innovation will carry greater institutional esteem and external credibility.

For students, this should mean more consistently thoughtful, inclusive and evidence-informed educational experiences, shaped by educators who have the autonomy and support to improve practice. Over time, King’s will become better at learning from its own educational work, directing attention and investment towards what makes the greatest difference, and contributing a clearer and more credible voice to wider debates about education.
transformationClaim: King's has a connected, evidence-led education culture in which educators and students can shape strategic priorities, develop and test practice, demonstrate impact, gain recognition and extend King's influence.

deliverables.title: Education Culture Infrastructure
deliverables.summary: Creates the central and faculty-level infrastructure needed to strengthen education culture consistently across King’s.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Establishes the institutional infrastructure through which education culture can be convened, supported and connected more consistently across King’s. King’s Academy would provide central coordination, helping to establish shared direction, connect activity across faculties and services, support institutional conversations and ensure that educational development is linked to wider strategic priorities.

Alongside this, the deliverable would establish a proportionate expectation that every faculty has recognisable education-development capacity to support the Vice Dean Education and local educational leadership. The model would respond to faculty context rather than require identical structures everywhere. In some faculties this may build on an existing education-development unit. Elsewhere it may involve dedicated coordination or administrative support, supplemented where necessary by centrally held strategic project capacity.

The aim is to give every faculty a practical route for convening its education community, supporting colleagues to develop and share practice, connecting local priorities to institutional opportunities and ensuring that strong educational work is not dependent on informal relationships or individual goodwill. The deliverable would also clarify the respective roles of King’s Academy, faculties, professional services and institutional governance, creating a connected operating model without centralising ownership of educational practice.

deliverables.title: Strategic Communities of Practice
deliverables.summary: Creates resourced and influential communities around strategic educational priorities, giving distributed educational leadership a visible home and enabling collective expertise, innovation and evidence to shape shared institutional practice.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Strategic Communities of Practice are institutionally recognised communities that sit between strategy and educational practice. Convened around selected priorities, which might initially include areas such as experiential learning, AI in education or inclusive education, they bring together academic and professional service educators, students and partners to develop shared understanding, connect ground-up innovation and co-own educational change. They provide a locus of energy and influence for educational leadership, while helping King’s develop greater collective resonance without imposing uniform solutions from the centre.

Each community combines a committed core with a wider, porous periphery, while agile shards undertake focused work around particular challenges or opportunities. Through events, collaborative inquiry, commissioned activity, seed funding, showcases and wider outreach, communities engage colleagues beyond existing enthusiasts, test and evidence emerging ideas, and help effective practice travel across the institution. Workloaded conveners sustain the community, while an Innovation Subcommittee provides strategic direction, a commissioning budget and light-touch accountability. Together, this creates a practical mechanism through which distinctive King’s provisions, practices and pedagogies can emerge from distributed expertise and genuine co-construction.

caseForChange.problem:
King’s has substantial educational expertise, leadership and innovation distributed across faculties, professional services and student communities. Much of this work is thoughtful and ambitious, but it can remain locally bounded, weakly connected to related activity elsewhere and only loosely linked to institutional priorities. Promising ideas are therefore harder to discover, similar challenges may be addressed repeatedly, and opportunities for collective learning and wider influence are missed.

King’s also faces a challenge of coherence. The university increasingly needs to identify common points of resonance across its educational provision while preserving disciplinary difference, local agency and professional judgement. Central policy and standardisation can create consistency, but they can also become overly directive and generate compliance rather than shared ownership. King’s currently lacks a sufficiently influential middle layer through which common approaches can be developed collaboratively from practice.
caseForChange.opportunity:
Strategic Communities of Practice offer King’s a mechanism for distributed educational leadership. They create recognised spaces in which educators, students, professional services and partners can interpret shared challenges, connect local expertise and shape institutional responses together. In doing so, they provide a more visible and resourced home for educational leadership and a stronger route through which practice can inform strategy.

Sitting between strategy and practice, the communities can identify areas of common resonance, surface and connect promising work, convene sustained educational conversations, stimulate innovation and generate evidence for wider development. They also provide a way to steward investment more coherently, using light-touch governance and commissioning resource to support activity that has both local value and wider institutional relevance.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is renewing its focus on education as a field of academic leadership and on the development of credible, rewarding education-focused career pathways. This creates an opportunity to give colleagues a stronger locus for educational contribution, identity and influence, while connecting individual development with wider institutional improvement. It also creates a timely context for strengthening the visibility and esteem of educational leadership across the university.

At the same time, the university is seeking to add more deliberate value to educational practice in ways that enhance student outcomes and experiences. The Education Delivery Framework establishes a number of common themes around which provision across King’s can find greater collective resonance. These themes now need spaces in which they can be interpreted, challenged and developed by the people who teach, support and learn at King’s, rather than translated too quickly into centrally determined solutions.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s develops a repeatable model through which strategic educational priorities are shaped by distributed leadership and collective expertise. Colleagues gain visible, resourced and influential spaces in which to develop their practice, contribute beyond their immediate setting and shape wider educational direction. Ground-up innovation becomes easier to identify, connect, support and learn from, while institutional investment is more coherently stewarded.

The Education Delivery Framework becomes a basis for shared inquiry and development rather than a mechanism for uniformity. Common provisions, practices and pedagogies can emerge where they add value, while remaining responsive to disciplinary context, local expertise and student experience. Over time, King’s becomes better able to combine institutional coherence with local agency, so that educational leadership gains greater visibility and esteem, promising practice travels more readily, and educational change is more genuinely shared, evidence-informed and enduring.

deliverables.title: Educational Impact, Evidence and Recognition
deliverables.summary: Builds a shared approach to evidencing educational impact and using that evidence to strengthen recognition, progression and esteem.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Creates a shared institutional approach to understanding the impact of educational work on students and the health of education culture at King’s. It would bring together measures that already exist across the institution into a more coherent evaluation framework, helping colleagues ask what works, for whom, under what conditions and with what effect on student experience and outcomes.

The primary focus would be student impact, including satisfaction, academic achievement, career readiness, destinations, belonging, self-efficacy, wellbeing and engagement. Attendance and KEATS activity would provide important measures of institutional engagement, alongside broader participation in learning, services, enrichment and enhancement. Equity of impact would run through the framework, ensuring that aggregate improvement does not conceal uneven experience or outcomes between student groups.

The framework would be proportionate to the scale of the work being evaluated. Small exploratory interventions should be able to use light-touch methods, while larger institutional initiatives should be supported to develop stronger evaluation designs, baselines and comparative evidence. A central education impact and evaluation capability would help educators and projects use institutional data responsibly, develop theories of impact and make informed decisions about what should grow, change or stop.

Alongside student impact, the deliverable would establish a light-touch account of education culture itself. This could include patterns in education-focused promotion, participation in development opportunities and strategic communities, applications for innovation support, educational leadership activity and external engagement.

This stronger evidence base would also support recognition. Educators would have clearer ways to evidence their contribution and impact for progression, promotion and institutional recognition. This would include colleagues on academic education pathways and professional services educators whose directly educative work is often significant but less consistently visible. The deliverable would help turn educational contribution into credible narratives of leadership, impact and esteem, supported by practical opportunities such as recognised leadership roles, project secondments, bought-out time and development support.

deliverables.title: Innovation Incubation and External Influence
deliverables.summary: Creates a supported route through which promising educational ideas can be tested, developed, scaled and projected externally.
deliverables.detailSummary:
Establishes a coherent incubation model for educational innovation, giving promising ideas access to the time, resource, expertise and support needed to move from early exploration into credible practice. It would provide a visible route through which ideas emerging from faculties, services, Strategic Communities of Practice and student collaboration can be identified, tested, evaluated and developed.

The model would operate at more than one level. Small seed investments would support rapid experimentation, exploratory work and ideas that are not yet ready for substantial institutional commitment. A smaller number of strategic flagship interventions would receive more sustained support, stronger evaluation and clearer routes to institutional adoption or external profile. The approach would be open to innovation within the curriculum and in more agile co-curricular or extracurricular settings, including King’s Edge where appropriate.

Incubation would involve more than funding. It would connect innovators to learning design, project management, evaluation, data, student co-creation, partnership development and institutional decision routes. Promising practice would be supported to clarify the problem it addresses, test its assumptions, understand its impact and determine whether it should be scaled, embedded, adapted or concluded. Existing innovation funds could gradually be connected through this common framework, making investment more visible, strategically aligned and consistently evaluated.

The deliverable would also create deliberate routes for externalisation. King’s Academy would work with educators, communities and projects to turn credible practice and evidence into sector-facing outputs, including articles, reports, blogs, podcasts, conference contributions, partnerships, events and public conversations. The Policy Institute would act as a standing partner, helping connect educational practice to policy thinking and translate evaluated institutional experience into sector and government-facing influence.

The ambition is for King’s to build an external voice grounded in implemented practice and credible evidence. Educational innovation would therefore contribute not only to internal improvement, but also to institutional reputation, sector leadership and wider policy influence.

title: Curriculum Framework and Review
summary: Provides the route through which King's Edge requirements are embedded in curriculum audit, review and enhancement.
edgeRole: Carries the curriculum audit and review mechanism that surfaces skills, experiential learning, graduate premium contribution and growth priorities.

title: Student Experience Hub
summary: Creates a coherent and personalised digital student experience by progressively integrating existing systems and platforms, strengthening the CRM capability that connects services behind them, and developing digital enhancements within a shared institutional environment.
detailSummary:
The Student Experience Hub will provide a single, evolving digital environment through which students can discover information and opportunities, access services, complete actions, seek support and follow progress. Rather than replacing all existing systems at once, it will progressively integrate their information and functionality into clearer student journeys, reducing reliance on disconnected portals and routes.

Strengthened CRM capability will connect the services behind this experience, providing shared context, knowledge, workflows and case management so that interactions can be coordinated across teams and channels. Students should receive more consistent support, greater visibility of progress and less need to repeat information, while staff gain clearer ownership and understanding of the student journey.

A multidisciplinary Digital Innovation Development Team will evolve the Hub iteratively by integrating priority systems, testing digital enhancements and maturing successful features for sustained institutional use. This will include meeting the demands generated by King’s Edge—such as opportunity discovery, personalised curation, planning, participation, evidence and recognition—within capabilities designed for reuse across the wider student experience.

Over time, the Hub will bring CRM investment, platform integration and digital innovation into one coherent product direction, enabling King’s to continuously improve a more personalised, intelligent and responsive student experience.

caseForChange.problem:
King’s students currently experience the university through a fragmented collection of systems, portals and service channels. Information, opportunities, administrative tasks and support are distributed according to institutional structures rather than organised around student needs. Students must work out where to go, navigate inconsistent interfaces and often move between several systems or teams to complete a single journey.

This fragmentation continues behind the student-facing experience. Information about enquiries and interactions is not consistently shared; knowledge, routing and workflows vary between services; and ownership of next steps may be unclear. Students can be redirected, lose visibility of progress or have to repeat their circumstances, while staff may lack the context needed to provide timely and coordinated support.

King’s also lacks a sufficiently coherent route for meeting new digital needs. Requirements are liable to be addressed through local system changes, project-specific portals or separate products, reproducing common functions and expanding the digital estate without making the student experience more joined up.
caseForChange.opportunity:
The Student Experience Hub creates the opportunity to bring the student-facing experience, the institutional service infrastructure behind it and the development of new digital capabilities into one evolving model.

The app can progressively integrate existing systems and platforms, surfacing relevant information and functionality through coherent student journeys rather than requiring students to navigate each underlying system separately. This allows King’s to build from what already exists, rationalise duplicated entry points over time and develop new capabilities only where current provision is absent or inadequate.

CRM investment can provide the shared context, knowledge, workflow and case-management capability through which King’s responds when students take action or seek support. It connects the visible digital experience with the services and teams responsible for fulfilling it, enabling clearer ownership, better routing and more consistent follow-through.

A Digital Innovation Development Team can then evolve the Hub continuously. Working within shared architecture, standards and governance, it can integrate services, prototype new journeys and develop digital enhancements directly within the common environment. Requirements generated by King’s Edge—including opportunity discovery, personalised curation, planning, participation, evidence and recognition—can be met through this model, with the underlying capabilities designed for wider reuse rather than enclosed within a separate Edge product.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is at an inflection point.

Student frustration with fragmented systems, poor communication and difficult service journeys is already pronounced. Existing strategic work identifies both a unified digital front door and improved CRM-enabled enquiry management as high institutional priorities, reflecting the limits of continued local or incremental fixes.

At the same time, significant decisions are approaching about CRM investment, system integration and the future student app. King’s Edge is also moving into delivery and will generate substantial new demand for digital functionality, while early portal work has begun to establish elements of the architecture and development culture needed for in-house development.

These pressures are converging. Unless they are aligned before investment and development accelerate, King’s risks committing to separate CRM, app and programme-specific solutions that reproduce the fragmentation it is trying to resolve. The full end state will necessarily be delivered in phases, but the shared direction, ownership and architectural foundations need to be established now.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s moves from a fragmented collection of student-facing systems and service channels to a single, continuously evolving digital relationship with students.

The Student Experience Hub becomes the primary environment through which students discover information and opportunities, complete actions, access services and seek support. Existing platforms remain authoritative where appropriate, but their information and functionality are progressively integrated into coherent journeys, reducing duplicated entry points and the need for students to understand internal organisational structures.

Behind the Hub, strengthened CRM, knowledge and workflow capabilities connect the services responsible for fulfilling those journeys. Staff work from clearer shared context, enquiries have visible ownership and progress, and students are less likely to be redirected, lose track of an issue or repeat their circumstances across multiple teams.

New digital enhancements are developed within this same environment rather than through additional standalone products. A multidisciplinary development team integrates systems, tests new ideas and matures successful prototypes into reliable institutional capabilities. This includes delivering the feature requirements generated by King’s Edge while designing those capabilities for wider use across the student experience.

The result is a reinforcing model in which CRM investment, system integration and digital innovation contribute to one coherent product direction. Each new development improves the shared student experience rather than adding another layer to the digital estate.

deliverables.title: Student Relationship and CRM Foundation
deliverables.summary: Establishes the shared CRM, knowledge, workflow and case-management capability needed to coordinate student interactions across services and channels.
deliverables.detailSummary:
The Student Relationship and CRM Foundation will create the institutional service infrastructure needed to manage student enquiries, interactions and support more coherently. It will strengthen and extend CRM capability so that relevant context, knowledge, ownership and progress can be shared across the central and faculty teams involved in a student journey.

The deliverable will establish common approaches to enquiry capture, triage, routing, escalation and case management, supported by maintained knowledge content and integrations with core student systems. This will enable students to move more smoothly between self-service, general advice and specialist support without repeatedly navigating organisational boundaries or supplying the same information.

The work provides the service foundation beneath the Student Experience Hub but does not encompass the physical construction and operation of Student Centres or the complete institutional staffing model for student services. It focuses on the digital, data and workflow capability required to make a joined-up service possible.

caseForChange.problem:
Student enquiries and interactions are currently managed through a mixture of systems, channels and locally defined processes. Information is not always available across the teams involved in a journey; routing and escalation arrangements vary; and ownership of an enquiry or next action can be unclear.

Students may consequently be redirected between services, repeat their circumstances, receive inconsistent responses or lose visibility of progress. Staff may have only a partial understanding of previous interactions and can spend time reconstructing context, identifying the correct service or compensating for gaps between systems and processes.

The absence of a sufficiently shared relationship-management foundation therefore affects both student experience and operational effectiveness. It prevents the institution from consistently coordinating support around the student rather than around service boundaries.
caseForChange.opportunity:
Investment in CRM provides an opportunity to establish a shared institutional foundation for managing student relationships.

Common information, knowledge, workflows and case-management practices can connect the teams responsible for responding to students. Enquiries can be captured consistently, directed to the appropriate owner, progressed visibly and escalated when necessary. Integration with authoritative student systems can give staff the context required to act while maintaining appropriate access and data controls.

This would support a genuinely omnichannel model: students could begin through the Student Experience Hub or another appropriate channel while the underlying relationship and workflow remain coherent. The opportunity is not simply to install or extend software, but to use CRM investment to establish clearer service ownership and more consistent fulfilment across King’s.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is at an inflection point in its investment in student services and digital infrastructure.

Poor administration, fragmented enquiry routes and inconsistent communication are already persistent sources of student frustration. At the same time, significant CRM investment and service-model decisions are approaching, with dependencies on wider student-systems, data and institutional transformation work.

Choices made now will shape how student interactions are managed for several years. Continuing to extend CRM through disconnected service-specific workflows would embed existing fragmentation and make later integration more difficult. Establishing common principles, architecture and ownership alongside the next phase of investment allows King’s to build a shared foundation from the outset.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s has a shared digital foundation through which student enquiries, interactions and support needs can be coordinated across services and channels.

Priority student journeys operate through agreed workflows with clear ownership, routing and escalation. Staff can access appropriate shared context and maintained knowledge, while students receive more consistent responses, have greater visibility of progress and are less frequently required to repeat information.

Over time, more services and faculty teams adopt the common foundation, enabling King’s to manage its relationship with students as a connected institutional service rather than a collection of separate enquiry functions.

benefits.title: Students receive coordinated support without repeatedly telling their story
benefits.statement: Relevant context follows the student across appropriate services and channels, reducing repeated explanation, unnecessary redirection and conflicting responses.
benefits.beneficiary: Students, particularly those with complex or multi-service needs
benefits.realisationPeriod: Progressive from the first integrated priority journeys
benefits.successLooksLike: Students experience continuity between teams and report less repetition and avoidable handoff.

benefits.title: Enquiries are resolved more quickly and transparently
benefits.statement: Clear routing, ownership, service expectations and visible case progress reduce delays and make it easier for students and staff to understand what happens next.
benefits.realisationPeriod: From implementation of priority workflows, expanding with service adoption
benefits.successLooksLike: More enquiries are resolved at the appropriate first point, avoidable transfers decline and students can see meaningful progress.

benefits.title: Services work with greater coordination and operational insight
benefits.statement: Shared workflows and interaction data allow services to coordinate activity, identify recurring pain points and improve provision using a more complete view of student demand.
benefits.beneficiary: Central services, faculties and institutional leaders
benefits.realisationPeriod: As adoption and data quality mature
benefits.successLooksLike: Teams use shared information to improve routing, knowledge and service design, with less duplicated handling and fewer repeat enquiries.

deliverables.title: Digital Front Door and Journey Integration
deliverables.summary: Creates the evolving digital front door through which students can navigate information, opportunities, actions and support across King’s.
deliverables.detailSummary:
The Digital Front Door and Journey Integration deliverable will establish the Student Experience Hub as the primary digital environment through which students navigate their relationship with King’s. It will progressively bring together relevant information and functionality from existing systems, organising the experience around student journeys rather than institutional structures.

The Hub will be developed iteratively. Priority journeys and systems will be integrated in successive phases, combining common navigation, search, personalisation, self-service and access to support. Existing systems will remain authoritative where appropriate, but students should increasingly be able to complete useful parts of their journey within the Hub rather than being passed between unrelated destinations.

This approach avoids both an unmanageable wholesale replacement programme and the creation of another superficial portal containing only links. It creates a sustained product through which duplicated entry points can be rationalised and the student experience improved over time.

caseForChange.problem:
Students currently encounter King’s through a fragmented collection of portals, applications, websites and organisational routes. Information and functionality are structured around the systems and teams that provide them rather than around what a student is trying to achieve.

Students must therefore know where to look, interpret inconsistent terminology and interfaces, and move between multiple destinations to complete related actions. Important services and opportunities may remain invisible unless students already understand the institution or happen to encounter the correct communication.

This creates unnecessary effort and uneven access. It also weakens the value of the substantial services, information and opportunities King’s already provides because their breadth is not matched by a coherent way of navigating and using them.
caseForChange.opportunity:
The Student Experience Hub can create a common student-facing experience across existing platforms without requiring every underlying system to be replaced.

Information and functionality can be progressively integrated into coherent end-to-end journeys. Common capabilities such as navigation, search, personalisation, notifications, self-service and case visibility can be developed once and reused across different areas of the student experience.

An iterative approach allows King’s to prioritise the journeys where integration will make the greatest difference, learn from student use and progressively rationalise duplicated destinations. The result can become increasingly useful and intelligent over time rather than depending on a single large launch.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is at an inflection point in the future of its student-facing digital estate.

The current fragmentation is already a material source of student frustration, while the institution is considering the future of existing student apps, portals and integration approaches. The Digital Front Door has been identified as a high-priority initiative, including an expectation that parallel entry points will progressively be rationalised.

At the same time, CRM investment and new digital demands are creating decisions that need a common student-facing product direction. Without that direction, individual programmes and services are likely to continue developing separate destinations and interfaces that later require integration or replacement.

The full environment cannot be delivered at once, but its architectural direction and product ownership need to be established before further investment compounds the current fragmentation.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
The Student Experience Hub becomes the primary and continuously evolving digital front door to King’s.

Students can increasingly discover relevant information and opportunities, complete actions, access services and seek support through journeys designed around their needs. Existing systems continue to hold authoritative data and specialist functionality, but their useful information and actions are surfaced through a more coherent experience.

Priority journeys are integrated iteratively, personalisation improves as the underlying data and capabilities mature, and duplicated entry points are progressively reduced. Students become less dependent on understanding King’s internal structures in order to make effective use of the university.

benefits.title: Students can navigate King’s more easily
benefits.statement: Students can find relevant information, services and actions through coherent journeys rather than identifying and navigating separate institutional systems.
benefits.realisationPeriod: From the first released priority journeys, increasing with integration
benefits.successLooksLike: Students can complete common journeys with fewer destinations, fewer dead ends and less dependence on prior institutional knowledge.

benefits.title: Access to provision becomes more equitable
benefits.statement: Personalised discovery and clearer digital routes make services and opportunities more visible to students who are less able to rely on informal knowledge, confidence or existing networks.
benefits.beneficiary: Students, particularly those currently less well served by fragmented provision
benefits.realisationPeriod: As discovery, personalisation and priority content mature
benefits.successLooksLike: Relevant provision reaches a broader range of students and differences in discovery or take-up between student groups begin to narrow.

benefits.title: King’s reduces duplication in its student-facing digital estate
benefits.statement: Shared product capabilities and progressive integration reduce the need for services and programmes to maintain parallel entry points and repeated versions of common functions.
benefits.owner: TBC – Digital product and architecture owner
benefits.realisationPeriod: Medium term, as systems and journeys are integrated
benefits.successLooksLike: New requirements are met through the Hub where appropriate, and redundant portals or entry points can be retired without loss of necessary functionality.

deliverables.title: Digital Innovation and Product Development Capability
deliverables.summary: Creates the multidisciplinary capability to rapidly design, test and mature digital enhancements within the shared Student Experience Hub.
deliverables.detailSummary:
The Digital Innovation and Product Development Capability will establish a sustained route through which emerging student needs can become working digital enhancements. A multidisciplinary team will combine product management, service design, user research, software development, data, integration and change expertise around the continued evolution of the Student Experience Hub.

The team will work iteratively: understanding a need, prototyping a response, testing it with students and services, and developing successful approaches into reliable capabilities. Work will take place within the Hub’s shared architecture and standards so that innovation strengthens the common environment rather than generating additional standalone products.

The deliverable will also establish a deliberate route from experimentation into sustained operation. Capabilities that prove valuable will be hardened, documented and transferred into appropriate enduring ownership, while unsuccessful approaches can be concluded before they generate unnecessary long-term commitments.

caseForChange.problem:
King’s does not currently have a sufficiently dependable route for turning emerging student-experience needs into well-designed digital capabilities.

Requirements are often addressed through conventional system change, local initiatives, temporary projects or standalone products. These routes can be slow, difficult to prioritise and disconnected from one another. Similar functions may be developed more than once, while promising prototypes can struggle to move into secure and supported institutional operation.

This limits King’s ability to respond rapidly to changing student expectations and new strategic propositions. It also means innovation can expand the digital estate without strengthening a coherent shared environment.
caseForChange.opportunity:
A dedicated multidisciplinary product-development capability can give King’s a faster and more disciplined route from need to sustainable digital enhancement.

Working directly within the Student Experience Hub, the team can combine discovery, service design, prototyping, technical development and evaluation. It can test assumptions early, involve students continuously and reuse shared capabilities, data and integrations.

A clear maturation and handover model would allow experimentation without creating unsupported permanent products. Successful enhancements can become part of the institutional service, while evidence from unsuccessful or partial trials can still inform future decisions.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s is at an inflection point in both its digital architecture and its demand for innovation.

The Student Experience Hub and CRM investments will establish foundations that could support a more agile development model, but only if product practices, standards and delivery capacity are created alongside them. King’s Edge is also generating an immediate pipeline of substantial feature requirements that cannot wait for the completion of a multi-year end-state platform.

Without a shared development capability, this demand is likely to be channelled into separate procurements, local builds or temporary products. Establishing the capability now allows early requirements to test and strengthen the Hub’s architecture while producing visible student value.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s has a sustained multidisciplinary capability for continuously developing its digital student experience.

Emerging needs enter a transparent product pipeline, are explored with students and service owners, and are tested through proportionate prototypes before substantial commitment. Successful enhancements are developed within the shared Hub environment and transferred into secure, supported ownership.

Digital innovation becomes faster without becoming fragmented: each development either extends reusable institutional capability or generates evidence that improves future decisions.

benefits.title: King’s responds more quickly to emerging student needs
benefits.statement: Priority needs can be translated into tested digital responses in shorter cycles, with student feedback shaping development before large commitments are made.
benefits.owner: TBC – Digital Innovation Development Team product lead
benefits.realisationPeriod: From establishment of the first delivery pipeline
benefits.successLooksLike: Priority needs move predictably from discovery to tested capability, and users can influence solutions while they are still adaptable.

benefits.title: Digital investment creates more reusable institutional value
benefits.statement: New enhancements are built using common architecture and capabilities, reducing repeated development and allowing value created for one journey to benefit others.
benefits.beneficiary: King’s, product teams, services and students
benefits.owner: TBC – Digital product and architecture owner
benefits.realisationPeriod: Increasing as the shared capability library develops
benefits.successLooksLike: New work reuses existing components and integrations, and capabilities developed for one proposition are demonstrably adopted elsewhere.

benefits.title: Successful innovation becomes sustainable institutional provision
benefits.statement: Valuable prototypes have a defined route into secure, supported and appropriately owned operation rather than remaining temporary or becoming unmanaged legacy products.
benefits.owner: TBC – Student Experience Hub product owner
benefits.realisationPeriod: From the first capability-maturation and transfer cycle
benefits.successLooksLike: Successful enhancements have explicit support, security, maintenance and ownership arrangements, while unsuccessful trials can be closed cleanly.

deliverables.title: King’s Edge Digital Experience
deliverables.summary: Delivers the digital experience through which students can discover, pursue, evidence and gain recognition for King’s Edge opportunities within the shared Student Experience Hub.
deliverables.detailSummary:
The King’s Edge Digital Experience will deliver the student-facing capabilities needed to make the King’s Edge proposition navigable, personalised and usable. Students will be able to discover relevant opportunities, connect them to their aspirations, plan participation, manage activity, capture evidence, reflect on development and gain recognition for what they have achieved.

These requirements will be delivered within the Student Experience Hub rather than through a separate long-term Edge portal. Capabilities such as discovery, recommendation, planning, participation, communication, evidence and recognition will be designed for reuse across the wider student experience, while King’s Edge retains ownership of its proposition, content, curation and development model.

King’s Edge will provide a substantial early body of product demand through which the Hub’s architecture and innovation model can be tested. The result should deliver immediate value to Edge while accelerating shared capabilities that can subsequently support other student journeys.

caseForChange.problem:
King’s offers a wide range of experiences, activities and development opportunities, but students cannot currently navigate them as a coherent developmental journey.

Opportunities are distributed across providers, systems and communications. Students may struggle to discover what is relevant, understand how different experiences connect to their aspirations, or maintain a useful record of what they have done and learned. Access therefore depends too heavily on prior knowledge, confidence and networks.

King’s Edge will intensify the need for digital discovery, personalisation, planning, participation, reflection, evidence and recognition. Without a shared route for delivering these functions, Edge risks creating another standalone destination and duplicating capabilities that are also valuable elsewhere.
caseForChange.opportunity:
King’s Edge can become a major early use case for the Student Experience Hub and its digital innovation capability.

The demands generated by Edge can be translated into an integrated student journey: from exploring purpose and aspirations, through discovering and participating in relevant opportunities, to capturing evidence and articulating development. This creates a tangible student proposition while testing shared capabilities against substantive real-world needs.

Designing the resulting functions for wider reuse means investment made to support Edge can also strengthen the broader student experience. Edge remains distinctive through its opportunity offer, developmental framework, curation and recognition, while the underlying digital capabilities become part of the common institutional environment.
caseForChange.whyNow:
King’s Edge is moving from proposition into delivery and needs credible digital enablement to achieve its intended reach and coherence.

Early portal development has already begun to establish elements of the architecture and development culture required for in-house delivery. Decisions taken now will determine whether this work becomes a separate Edge product or contributes to the shared Student Experience Hub.

The wider Hub will take time to integrate the full institutional landscape, but Edge requirements are sufficiently developed to provide an immediate and comparatively lower-risk route for testing new capabilities. Delaying alignment would either impede Edge delivery or create functions that later need to be migrated, redesigned or replaced.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
Students encounter King’s Edge as a coherent developmental experience within the Student Experience Hub.

They can explore their aspirations, discover relevant opportunities, make plans, participate, connect experiences to skills and goals, capture evidence, reflect and gain recognition. Their journey is not confined to one isolated programme interface but connects naturally with the wider information, services and opportunities available through King’s.

The capabilities developed to support Edge become reusable parts of the Hub. King’s Edge gains the digital functionality it needs, while the institution gains tested approaches to personalisation, planning, participation, evidence and recognition that can support other student journeys.

benefits.title: More students discover and access relevant opportunities
benefits.statement: A coherent and personalised route helps students find opportunities that fit their interests, circumstances, development needs and aspirations.
benefits.beneficiary: All students, particularly those less connected to informal opportunity networks
benefits.realisationPeriod: From release of discovery and recommendation capabilities
benefits.successLooksLike: A broader and more representative range of students discover and progress into relevant opportunities, with less reliance on chance or insider knowledge.

benefits.title: Students pursue development more intentionally
benefits.statement: Students can connect aspirations, plans and participation, helping them make more purposeful choices and sustain engagement across their time at King’s.
benefits.realisationPeriod: From implementation of planning and participation journeys
benefits.successLooksLike: Students use the environment to identify goals, select appropriate next steps and connect separate experiences into a meaningful developmental journey.

benefits.title: Students can evidence and articulate what they have developed
benefits.statement: Evidence capture, reflection and recognition help students understand and communicate the capabilities and impact developed through their King’s experience.
benefits.beneficiary: Students, graduates and prospective employers or partners
benefits.realisationPeriod: From implementation of evidence and recognition capabilities, strengthening over time
benefits.successLooksLike: Students maintain credible evidence of development, receive appropriate recognition and can articulate their skills and experience with greater confidence and specificity.
